title: Handling Late-Arriving Data in Streaming ETL
scenario: You have a streaming ETL pipeline (Apache Flink/Spark Streaming) processing user events. Events arrive late due to network latency. You need to calculate 5-minute event-time aggregation windows. How should you handle events that arrive after the window has closed?

options.text: Discard late events immediately to avoid polluting calculations. Log them as errors.
Use Watermarks with Allowed Lateness. Keep the window state in RocksDB for the allowed buffer time and emit updates when late events arrive.
Switch to processing-time windows instead of event-time windows so late events are simply grouped by arrival time.
Increase the window size to 2 hours so that all late events are naturally caught inside the same window.

explanations.A: Discarding events leads to data loss and incorrect analytics, which is unacceptable for billing or critical business events.
explanations.B: Correct. Watermarking defines how long the engine waits for late events. Combined with 'Allowed Lateness', Flink/Spark keeps window state open, updating the aggregates incrementally as late events arrive.
explanations.C: Processing-time ignores when events actually happened, distorting hourly/daily analytics (e.g. events from midnight appear at 8 AM).
explanations.D: Increasing window size ruins real-time responsiveness (5-min SLA becomes 2-hour SLA).

title: Avoiding Out-Of-Memory (OOM) in Large ETL Joins
scenario: You are joining a huge transaction fact table (10 billion rows) with a small reseller dimension table (5,000 rows) in an Apache Spark ETL job. The job is failing with Executor OutOfMemory (OOM) errors. What optimization should you apply?

options.text: Repartition both tables on the join key to redistribute the data across executors. O(n log n) shuffle.
Increase the spark.executor.memory config setting to 128GB to handle the shuffle in-memory.
Use a Broadcast Join (Map-Side Join). Broadcast the small reseller table to all executors, eliminating the need to shuffle the massive fact table.
Convert the dimension table to a parquet format and cache it prior to joining.

explanations.A: Shuffling 10 billion rows is extremely expensive and causes heavy disk spill, leading to performance issues and potential OOMs.
explanations.B: Brute-force memory scaling is expensive and doesn't solve the underlying data skew or shuffle bottleneck.
explanations.C: Correct. Since the dimension table is tiny (5,000 rows, a few KB), broadcasting it avoids the network shuffle of the 10-billion-row fact table entirely. Each worker performs a local join.
explanations.D: Caching or file format changes do not prevent Spark from having to shuffle the fact table, which is the main source of the OOM.

title: Designing a Idempotent ETL Data Load
scenario: Your daily ETL pipeline loads data into a Snowflake data warehouse. If the pipeline runs twice due to a scheduler retry, you get duplicate records. How should you design the ingestion layer to guarantee idempotency?

options.text: Execute a DELETE query for the date range before running the INSERT query (Delete-Insert pattern).
Use a staging table, merge it into the target table using a MERGE (UPSERT) statement based on a unique transaction ID, and clear staging.
Rely on Snowflake's automatic primary key unique constraints to block duplicate inserts.
Both A and B are acceptable idempotent patterns, depending on the overwrite requirements.

explanations.A: Delete-Insert is a classic batch idempotent pattern for partitions (e.g. daily overwrites).
explanations.B: Merge/Upsert is the standard pattern for upserting records by a unique key, ensuring retries update existing rows instead of inserting duplicates.
explanations.C: Snowflake does NOT enforce primary key uniqueness constraints during inserts; constraints are only metadata declarations.
explanations.D: Correct. Both patterns (Delete-Insert for time-series partitions, MERGE for upserts) are standard industry patterns to guarantee idempotency in warehouse loading.
